annotations.label: Binary number

anchor.snippet: A binary number is a number expressed in the base-2 numeral system

annotations.note: No dedicated 'binary number' type, but `Nat.digits 2 n` gives a natural number's base-2 representation.

annotations.label: Binary number as dyadic rational

anchor.snippet: A binary number may also refer to a rational number that has a finite representation

annotations.note: Mathlib has no dyadic-rational structure (the only 'dyadic' references are topological partitions in Urysohn's lemma).

annotations.label: Base-2 numeral system

anchor.snippet: The base-2 numeral system is a positional notation

annotations.note: `Nat.ofDigits`/`Nat.digits` formalize positional notation for any base, of which base 2 is a special case.

annotations.label: Bit

anchor.snippet: Each digit is referred to as a bit

annotations.note: `Nat.bits : ℕ → List Bool` represents the binary digits as Booleans, with `Nat.testBit` giving the bit at a position.

annotations.label: Numbers representable by bit sequences

anchor.snippet: Any number can be represented by a sequence of bits

annotations.note: `ofDigits b (digits b n) = n` at base 2 expresses that every natural number is recovered from its bit sequence.

annotations.label: Equivalent binary notations

anchor.snippet: The following notations are equivalent

annotations.note: Purely notational equivalence (subscripts/prefixes), not a mathematical statement formalizable in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Reading binary digit-by-digit

anchor.snippet: the binary numeral 100 is pronounced one zero zero

annotations.note: Concerns pronunciation conventions, which carry no formalizable content.

annotations.label: Decimal counting symbols

anchor.snippet: Decimal counting uses the ten symbols 0 through 9

annotations.note: An informal statement about decimal symbols; only the related bound `Nat.digits_lt_base` exists, not this claim.

annotations.label: Binary counting procedure

anchor.snippet: Binary counting follows the exact same procedure

annotations.note: The incrementing/counting procedure on digit strings is not formalized as such (only `Nat.succ` on the numbers).

annotations.label: Binary place value as powers of 2

anchor.snippet: each bit represents an increasing power of 2

annotations.note: `ofDigits b L = Σ aᵢ·bⁱ` is exactly place value as powers of the base, specializing to 2ⁱ at base 2.

annotations.label: Convert 100101 to decimal

anchor.snippet: the binary number 100101 is converted to decimal form as follows

annotations.note: This specific conversion is an invocation of `Nat.ofDigits 2`, computable but not a stored declaration.

annotations.label: Binary addition of single digits

anchor.snippet: Adding two single-digit binary numbers is relatively simple

annotations.note: Addition itself is fully formalized; `bodd (m+n) = bxor (bodd m) (bodd n)` captures the single-bit sum rule, but the carry table is not a named result.

annotations.label: Carrying in addition

anchor.snippet: This is known as carrying

annotations.note: Carry is implicit in `Nat.add` but not defined as a standalone concept in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Adding 01101 and 10111

anchor.snippet: In this example, two numerals are being added together

annotations.note: A worked hand-addition-with-carries example; the carrying algorithm on digit strings is not formalized.

annotations.label: XOR addition rule

anchor.snippet: When computers must add two numbers

annotations.note: `bodd (m+n) = bxor (bodd m) (bodd n)` formalizes the sum bit as XOR, though the carry-as-AND half-adder rule is not stated.

annotations.label: Long carry method

anchor.snippet: Brookhouse Method of Binary Addition

annotations.note: This human addition shortcut for long carry runs is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Long carry addition example

anchor.snippet: In the following example, two numerals are being added together

annotations.note: A worked example of the long-carry method, which is not formalized.

annotations.label: Addition table vs disjunction

anchor.snippet: The binary addition table is similar to, but not the same as, the truth table of the logical disjunction operation

annotations.note: The observation that bit addition (XOR with carry) differs from logical OR is not stated as a Mathlib result.

annotations.label: Binary subtraction of single digits

anchor.snippet: Subtraction works in much the same way

annotations.note: Subtraction is formalized as `Nat.sub`/integer subtraction, but the single-digit borrow table is not a named declaration.

annotations.label: Borrowing in subtraction

anchor.snippet: This is known as borrowing

annotations.note: Borrowing is not formalized as a defined concept in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Two's complement subtraction formula

anchor.snippet: subtraction can be summarized by the following formula

annotations.note: Mathlib has no two's-complement representation or this subtraction formula.

annotations.label: Binary multiplication by partial products

anchor.snippet: Multiplication in binary is similar to its decimal counterpart

annotations.note: `n * ofDigits b l = ofDigits b (l.map (n*·))` captures distributing a multiplier over digits, the core of the partial-products idea.

annotations.label: Partial product outcomes

anchor.snippet: there are only two possible outcomes of each partial multiplication

annotations.note: The observation that multiplying by a bit yields 0 or the multiplicand is not stated as a Mathlib lemma.

annotations.label: Multiply 1011 by 1010

anchor.snippet: the binary numbers 1011 and 1010 are multiplied as follows

annotations.note: A worked hand-multiplication example; the digit-wise partial-products algorithm is not formalized.

annotations.label: Multiplication table vs conjunction

anchor.snippet: The binary multiplication table is the same as the truth table of the logical conjunction operation

annotations.note: No declaration states a·b = a∧b for single bits; only the related `Nat.and_two_pow` exists for bitwise AND.

annotations.label: Binary long division

anchor.snippet: Long division in binary is again similar to its decimal counterpart

annotations.note: Division is formalized (`Nat.div`); dividing by a power of the base equals dropping low digits, but the long-division algorithm itself is not formalized.

annotations.label: Divide 11011 by 101

anchor.snippet: In the example below, the divisor is

annotations.note: A worked binary long-division example; the algorithm is not formalized.

annotations.label: Binary square root algorithm

anchor.snippet: The process of taking a binary square root digit by digit

annotations.note: `Nat.sqrt` computes the integer square root but uses a different (non digit-by-digit) algorithm.

annotations.label: Terminating binary fraction condition

anchor.snippet: the binary expansion of a fraction terminates only if the denominator is a power of 2

annotations.note: No Mathlib theorem characterizes terminating base-2 expansions via power-of-2 denominators.

annotations.label: 1/10 has no finite binary form

anchor.snippet: 1/10 does not have a finite binary representation

annotations.note: This specific non-terminating instance is not formalized.

annotations.label: Binary expansion of 1/3

anchor.snippet: the binary expansion of 1/3 is .010101

annotations.note: The repeating binary expansion of 1/3 is not formalized as a stored result.

annotations.label: Bitwise operation

anchor.snippet: it is called a bitwise operation

annotations.note: `Nat.bitwise (f : Bool → Bool → Bool)` applies an operation bit-by-bit; `Nat.land`/`Nat.lor`/`Nat.xor` are concrete instances.

annotations.label: Arithmetic shift left as multiplication

anchor.snippet: an arithmetic shift left of a binary number is the equivalent of multiplication

annotations.note: `m <<< n = m * 2 ^ n` is exactly shift-left equals multiplication by a power of two.

annotations.label: Decimal to binary by division

anchor.snippet: the number is divided by two

annotations.note: `digits b n = n % b :: digits b (n / b)` is precisely the repeated-division-with-remainder algorithm, at base 2.

annotations.label: Convert 357 to binary

anchor.snippet: is expressed as

annotations.note: A specific conversion computable via `Nat.digits 2 357`, not a stored declaration.

annotations.label: Binary to decimal algorithm

anchor.snippet: Conversion from base-2 to base-10 simply inverts the preceding algorithm

annotations.note: `ofDigits b (digits b n) = n` formalizes that `ofDigits` inverts `digits`, specializing to base 2.

annotations.label: Convert 10010101101 to decimal

anchor.snippet: to convert 10010101101

annotations.note: A specific conversion that is an invocation of `Nat.ofDigits 2`.

annotations.label: Horner scheme application

anchor.snippet: This method is an application of the Horner scheme

annotations.note: `ofDigits b L = foldr (fun x y => x + b*y) 0 L` is the Horner-form evaluation, though it is not labeled as Horner's scheme.

annotations.label: Fractional conversion by doubling

anchor.snippet: Repeatedly double the number to be converted

annotations.note: `Real.digits` extracts digits of a real in [0,1) via floors, but the repeated-doubling algorithm is not itself a theorem.

annotations.label: Repeating fraction 0.3 in binary

anchor.snippet: Thus the repeating decimal fraction

annotations.note: This specific repeating binary fraction is not formalized.

annotations.label: 0.1 in binary

anchor.snippet: Or for example, 0.1

annotations.note: The binary expansion of 0.1 is not formalized as a stored result.

annotations.label: Form of terminating binary fractions

anchor.snippet: the only binary fractions with terminating expansions are of the form of an integer divided by a power of 2

annotations.note: No theorem characterizes terminating binary fractions as integer/2ᵏ (dyadic rationals).

annotations.label: Divide-and-conquer conversion

anchor.snippet: A simple divide-and-conquer algorithm is more effective asymptotically

annotations.note: The asymptotically faster divide-and-conquer base-conversion algorithm is not formalized.

annotations.label: Four bits per hex digit

anchor.snippet: so it takes four digits of binary to represent one digit of hexadecimal

annotations.note: No Mathlib lemma relates base-b^k digits to groups of k base-b digits (here 16 = 2⁴).

annotations.label: Hexadecimal to binary

anchor.snippet: simply substitute the corresponding binary digits

annotations.note: The digit-substitution grouping between hex and binary is not formalized.

annotations.label: Binary to hexadecimal

anchor.snippet: divide it into groups of four bits

annotations.note: Grouping bits into hex digits is not formalized.

annotations.label: Hexadecimal to decimal

anchor.snippet: multiply the decimal equivalent of each hexadecimal digit

annotations.note: A specific conversion that is an invocation of `Nat.ofDigits 16`.

annotations.label: Three bits per octal digit

anchor.snippet: octal uses a radix of 8, which is a power of two

annotations.note: No Mathlib lemma relates base-8 digits to groups of three bits (8 = 2³).

annotations.label: Octal to binary

anchor.snippet: Converting from octal to binary proceeds in the same fashion

annotations.note: The octal-to-binary digit-grouping substitution is not formalized.

annotations.label: Octal to decimal

anchor.snippet: And from octal to decimal

annotations.note: A specific conversion that is an invocation of `Nat.ofDigits 8`.

annotations.label: Representing non-integers with radix point

anchor.snippet: Non-integers can be represented by using negative powers

annotations.note: `Real.ofDigits` represents reals in [0,1) as Σ dᵢ·b^{-(i+1)}, i.e. via negative powers of the base.

annotations.label: Binary 11.01 equals 3.25

anchor.snippet: the binary number 11.01

annotations.note: The fractional part .01₂ = 1/4 is an invocation of `Real.ofDigits`; the combined integer-plus-fraction value is not a stored result.

annotations.label: Dyadic rationals terminate

anchor.snippet: have a terminating binary numeral

annotations.note: No theorem states that dyadic rationals have terminating binary expansions (no dyadic-rational structure exists).

annotations.label: Other rationals recur

anchor.snippet: Other rational numbers have binary representation, but instead of terminating, they recur

annotations.note: Eventual periodicity of base-b expansions of rationals is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Non-terminating non-recurring are irrational

anchor.snippet: Binary numerals that neither terminate nor recur represent irrational numbers

annotations.note: The characterization linking non-eventually-periodic expansions to irrationality is not formalized.

annotations.label: Patterned irrational binary number

anchor.snippet: does have a pattern, but it is not a fixed-length recurring pattern

annotations.note: This specific patterned-but-irrational example is not formalized.

annotations.label: Binary representation of sqrt 2

anchor.snippet: the square root of 2

annotations.note: The example's content (√2 is irrational, hence non-terminating/non-recurring) is `irrational_sqrt_two`, though its binary digits are not formalized.
